text: 2023 Multi digit Payment Schedule 2023 Multi digit Spanish Payment Schedule 2023 Multi digit Chinese Payment Schedule 2022 Disbursement and Corresponding Dates for Cash/SNAP Benefits 2022 Multi digit Payment Schedule 2022 Multi digit Spanish Payment Schedule 2022 Multi digit Chinese Payment Schedule 2021 Disbursement and Corresponding Dates for Cash/SNAP Benefits 2021 Multi digit Payment Schedule 2021 Multi digit Spanish Payment Schedule 2020 Disbursement and Corresponding Dates for Cash/SNAP Benefits 2020 Multi digit Payment Schedule 2020 Multi digit Spanish Payment Schedule 2019 Disbursement and Corresponding Dates for Cash/SNAP Benefits

title: 580_Appendix B
text: 2019 Multi digit Payment Schedule 2019 Multi digit Spanish Payment Schedule Issuance of client benefits varies by county. The last digit of the seven-digit case record number determines the payment-day cycle number. Counties may have one, two, or ten payment-day cycles. - Beaver County is a one-payment-day cycle county. All recipients receive their benefits on payment cycle 3, regardless of the last digit of the case record number. - Berks County is a two-payment-day cycle county. Recipients with case record numbers ending in 1 through 5 receive their benefits on payment-day cycle 4. Recipients with case record numbers ending in 6 through 0 receive their benefits on payment-day cycle 9.

title: 580_Appendix B
text: - Lancaster County is a 10-payment-day cycle county. Recipients receive their benefits on the payment-day cycle that is the same as the last digit of the case record number. If the last digit of the seven digit case record number is: The corresponding payment day cycle is:

title: 580_Appendix C
text: Household Requests Replacement For: Benefits are returned at 9 months of inactivity and cannot be replaced. Replacement of food destroyed in a household disaster* The CAO must determine that food was destroyed. Food must have been purchased with SNAP benefits. The disaster must be verified. Replacement must be requested within 10 days of the disaster. The amount is limited to a maximum of one monthâs benefit. There is no limit on the number of replacements. ACCESS card replacement fee deducted in error If the fee is deducted in error, the CAO may authorize a replacement. *In cases where FNS has issued a disaster declaration, the household cannot receive both an emergency disaster benefit and a replacement benefit. Reissued December 15, 2022, replacing March 1, 2012

title: 580.5 Replacements
text: The electronic issuance of benefits through EBT helps make sure that recipients receive their benefits on the scheduled payment date. If an EBT card is lost or stolen, the recipient can protect his or her EBT benefits by calling the Conduent customer service number at 1-888-328-7366 and canceling the cards. The recipient may get benefits remaining in the account after getting a replacement EBT card from Conduent Customer Service or his or her CAO . The following are the only situation in which the CAO may issue replacement benefits to the household : - The EBT system returned benefits because the account was inactive. - The household lost food in a disaster. - The $2.50 ACCESS card replacement fee was deducted from the recipient's EBT account in error. There are no other times when replacement SNAP benefits may be issued. 7 CFR § 274.12(g)(5)(v) and 7 CFR § 274.6(a)

title: 580.5 Replacements
text: OPS-22-07-04 Changes to Benefit Expungement Timeframes for Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) and Cash Assistance Benefits, Issued July 19, 2022 There is no deadline or expiration date for EBT SNAP benefits. Benefits may accumulate in the recipientâs EBT account as long as the recipient keeps the account active by making at least one debit transaction every 274 days (9 months). If there is no debit activity for 274 days, the EBT account becomes inactive and the household's benefits are expunged and cannot be reissued. The system will issue a 452 option 3 notice to the SNAP household after 8 months of inactivity informing the client that if their card is not used at least once in the next 30 days the benefits will be expunged from that card. At nine months, the budget will be put in a suspended status. The household can contact the CAO to request the budget be returned to an active status, but expunged benefits cannot be reissued. If there is no contact from the client, the budget will remain in a suspended status until the next Semi-Annual Review (SAR) or renewal. If there is no response to the SAR or renewal, the budget can be closed. Example: A householdâs certification period is December 1, 2020, through November 30, 2021. The household does not use their SNAP benefits. The system sends the 452/3 in August 2021. The household continues to not use their SNAP benefits and their EBT card becomes inactive and benefits are expunged in September 2021. The client contacts 3 days after benefits are expunged and requests replacement. Benefits cannot be replaced. N OTE: Heat and Eat benefits cannot be reissued when they are expunged.

title: 580.5 Replacements
section_title: 580.52 Replacement of Food Destroyed in a Disaster Â
text: PFS-15802-580 Replacement SNAP Benefits for Food Lost in a Household Misfortune, Revised June 25, 2015 The CAO may replace food destroyed in a household misfortune or disaster if all of the following conditions are met: - The CAO determines that food was destroyed in a household misfortune or disaster, such as a fire or flood. - The food was bought with SNAP benefits. - The CAO verifies the household misfortune or disaster through a collateral contact , documentation from a community agency, a home visit ,newspaper articles, Pennsylvania Emergency Management Agency (PEMA) websites, and power company websites. The CAO should call the recipientâs power company to verify that the household was without power. If the CAO has made every attempt to verify the recipient was without power but unsuccessfully, they may take the clientâs statement. - The household requests the replacement orally or in writing within 10 days of the disaster and completes an Affidavit for Replacement of Food Destroyed in a Household Disaster ( PA 1896 English / PA 1896-S Spanish ) within 10 days of the reported food loss. NOTE: The replacement amount is limited to a maximum of one monthâs benefit, unless the issuance includes restored benefits which can also be replaced up to their full value. Reminder: There is no limit to the number of replacements for food lost because of a household misfortune or disaster. The CAO must use reason code 168 for replacement of food destroyed in a household misfortune or disaster. NOTE: A presidential or federal disaster declaration is not required to replace food lost in a household misfortune or disaster.

title: 580.5 Replacements
section_title: 580.53 ACCESS Card Replacement Fee Deducted in Error
text: Recipients may request a replacement for a lost, stolen, or damaged EBT ACCESS card. When the new card is issued, the EBT system deducts a card-replacement fee of $2.50 from the recipientâs cash account or, if there is no cash balance, from the recipientâs SNAP account. The CAO may waive the fee. If the fee is deducted in error, the CAO may authorize a replacement using reason code 183. CIS limits this authorization to no more than $10.00.

title: 580.6 Pennsylvania EBT ACCESS Card
text: The Pennsylvania EBT ACCESS card is an industry-standard plastic card with a magnetic stripe giving recipients access to cash assistance, SNAP benefits, or Medical Assistance benefits (or any combination of them). Recipients get SNAP benefits electronically through point-of-sale (POS) terminals in authorized food stores. They can get cash assistance through POS terminals and automated teller machines (ATMs). Recipients can verify their eligibility for Medical Assistance through the online Eligibility Verification System (EVS). The primary account number (PAN) is the 19-digit number printed on the front of the card. The PAN numbers are as follows: - The first six digits are the bank identification number (BIN) identifying the card as Pennsylvania EBT. - The next nine digits are the eCIS recipient number. - The 16th digit is the validation digit for the recipient number. - The 17th and 18th digits equal the card number that shows on CQIPAC screen. - The last digit is the PAN validation number.

title: 580.6 Pennsylvania EBT ACCESS Card
text: The CAO must issue the initial EBT ACCESS card to the household member designated as the payment name. Any client other than authorized representatives (ARs), secondary card members, and expedited SNAP recipients may request to have a card issued centrally. EBT ACCESS cards should be issued centrally except for Expedited SNAP benefits, Authorized Representative cards, secondary cards, emergency situations, or the card is damaged. The CAO must tell the client that it will take seven to ten days to receive a centrally issued EBT ACCESS card. The CAO must use the EBT administrative workstation or a desktop computer to produce recipientsâ EBT ACCESS cards. The workstation connects the CAO to the EBT system through the Commonwealth's network. This allows information to be transmitted between the EBT system and CIS and allows the CAO to make inquiries into the EBT system database. (For more information, see the Pennsylvania Electronic Benefit Transfer Xerox AT manual.) NOTE: EBT cards may be created locally and mailed to the client from the CAO in the following circumstances: Expedited SNAP and emergency situations. If the recipient is known to CIS, the workstation can produce a card. For new applicants, the CAO must first authorize benefits and CIS must assign a recipient number before an EBT card can be printed. CIS maintains a record of each card the CAO issues. The Xerox database also captures this information. - Issuance of a card to a secondary cardholder or AR

title: 580.6 Pennsylvania EBT ACCESS Card
text: There are three types of EBT cards: - A primary EBT card for the payment name Issuance of this card establishes the account on the EBT system. The CAO must always issue this card first. - A secondary card for another adult in the household The secondary cardholder must be an authorized and active member of the budget and be 18 years or older. This includes EE members The CAO must explain to the primary cardholder that the secondary cardholder has the same access to benefits as the primary cardholder. The CAO must get the primary cardholderâs approval before issuing the secondary card. The CAO must explain that the primary cardholder is responsible for the issued benefits if there is an overpayment. This allows a recipient who is unable to use the EBT system because of a disability to designate someone to access benefits. The recipient may authorize access to cash assistance or SNAP benefits or both. (See Chapter 505 .) The CAO may issue no more than two EBT ACCESS cards per householdâa primary card and, if it is requested, either a secondary card or an AR card.

title: 580.6 Pennsylvania EBT ACCESS Card
text: After the CAO prints an EBT ACCESS card for the recipient, the recipient must choose a personal identification number (PIN) using the Xerox PIN select . The PIN is a four-digit code that the cardholder must use to begin a transaction at either a POS or ATM . The PIN is the main means of security for the recipientâs EBT account. The PIN selection must be confidential. The CAO must provide an area where the recipient can select a PIN without being observed by others. The CAO must tell the recipient not to reveal the PIN to anyone because doing so would jeopardize the benefits in the account. The CAO must also remind recipients that they have three chances to enter the correct pin when using their EBT card for a purchase. After three wrong tries, the EBT system locks the card and prevents any transactions until the next day unless the recipient selects a new PIN at the CAO or by telephone. If recipients forget their PINs, they may call the Xerox customer service line at 1-888-328-7366 to select new PINs.

title: 580.6 Pennsylvania EBT ACCESS Card
text: DHS issues replacements for lost or stolen cards centrally. A $2.50 card-replacement fee is taken from the clientâs account. The CAO may issue a card locally if the client has an emergency justifying immediate replacement of a lost or stolen card. The executive director or his or her designate must approve the request. The CAO must replace a client's damaged EBT ACCESS card upon request, without charging a fee. The CAO must maintain the Daily EBT Card Log for a replacement that is not issued centrally (see Chapter 580, Appendix D ). NOTE: Even though a case may be closed, the recipient needs the replacement card to access benefits still in the account.

title: 580.6 Pennsylvania EBT ACCESS Card
text: The CAO must complete an EBT Card Request form and get the recipientâs signature on the form. The form must be kept in the case record. (See the sample in Chapter 580, Appendix A .) The CAO must monitor card replacements when the recipient requests multiple replacements for lost or stolen cards. After three replacements of a lost or stolen card within six months or two or more replacements in one month, the CAO must schedule the recipient for an interview with an OIM supervisor to discuss the recipientâs responsibility to keep the card safe. The OIM supervisor must tell the recipient that illegal use of the card may result in disqualification and that further replacements will result in a referral to the Office of Inspector General (OIG). The CAO must not deny or delay a recipient's request for replacement of the EBT ACCESS card.

title: 580.6 Pennsylvania EBT ACCESS Card
section_title: 580.642 Referrals to the Office of Inspector General
text: The CAO must make a referral to the OIG when: - The recipient asks for and gets more than one replacement of lost, stolen, or damaged cards in one month; or - The recipient asks for and gets more than four replacements of lost, stolen, or damaged cards in six months. The CAO must send card-replacement forms to the OIG at the address at the bottom of the form. A separate memorandum is not necessary. For more information on the EBT card replacement process, see the EBT Procedures Manual. NOTE: Only CAOs and district offices can replace the Pennsylvania EBT ACCESS cards. The CAO must apply a card-replacement fee as follows:

title: 580.6 Pennsylvania EBT ACCESS Card
text: - There is no cost to replace the EBT ACCESS card for recipients who reapply for cash assistance or SNAP benefits after a break in eligibility. - There is no cost to replace a damaged or defective card the recipient returns to the CAO. - There is no cost to replace cards issued to ARs. - The CAO must allow one replacement per payment name at no cost for cards lost or stolen during the period that the EBT account remains active. - The CAO must charge a $2.50 replacement fee for all other lost or stolen cards that are replaced. NOTE: The EBT system automatically deducts the card-replacement fee from the recipientâs EBT cash assistance or SNAP account. If the recipient has both a cash assistance and a SNAP account balance, the system deducts the $2.50 fee from the cash account. If the cash assistance balance is zero or the recipient has a SNAP-only account, the system deducts the fee from the SNAP account. NOTE: The CAO must never collect cash directly from the recipient for the EBT card-replacement fee. It must collect the fee through the EBT account. - The CAO must use the override option to waive the fee in the situations described above where there is no fee. The CAO must also waive the fee in exceptional circumstances or in cases with insufficient funds in the account if the recipient needs the replacement card to access medical services. NOTE: If the card-replacement fee is charged in error, the CAO must reimburse the recipient using supplemental benefit reason code 183.

title: 580.6 Pennsylvania EBT ACCESS Card
section_title: 580.65 Card-Issuance Situations and Availability of Benefits
text: Benefits are available and accessible by recipients on the EBT system when the following conditions are met: - There is an active EBT card; - The recipient enters a PIN on the Xerox PIN device; and - The authorized benefits are posted to the EBT account. Several situations can happen when the CAO creates EBT accounts and issues EBT ACCESS cards. Each situation has a different effect on the availability of EBT benefits. Here are the situations and effects: - The CAO authorizes the benefit, issues the EBT card, and the recipient selects a PIN, all on the same day. For applicants, the benefit is available at 6:00 a.m . the next calendar day. - The CAO authorizes the benefit on the first day, but the recipient does not get the card and PIN until the second day. The benefit is available after midnight on the second day. - The CAO authorizes the benefit and issues the EBT card on the first day, but the recipient does not choose a PIN on that day. An inquiry on the EBT system shows the card status as 00. This means the card has no PIN. (See the EBT administrative manual for other code meanings.) The recipient may come into the CAO or call the Xerox service line any time after that first day to select a PIN. The benefit is available within an hour after the recipient selects a PIN. - The CAO replaces an EBT card with benefits already in the account. The benefits are available as soon as the recipient chooses a PIN for the new card. If a card is replaced for an active account with a zero balance, benefits are available after 6:00 a.m. on the benefit- issuance date for that recipient. - The recipient chooses a new PIN for an existing card. The benefits are available immediately, and the block that was caused by three incorrect PIN entries is removed.

title: 580.7 Employment and Training Program (ETP)
text: The CAO may approve special allowances for supportive services for ETP participants to prepare for, seek, accept, or maintain education, training, or employment. The criteria and benefit amounts are the same for both cash assistance and SNAP recipients. (See Cash Assistance Handbook, Chapter 138 .) SNAP recipients cannot receive special allowances for meals. SNAP special allowances related to employment and training activities are processed as OTI through county disbursement for each month that the need exists. (See Cash Assistance Handbook .) Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing July 22, 2004

title: 581.10 Case Record Recording
text: When the CAO issues restored benefits , the case record must contain the following information: - The computation of the amount of restored benefits - The balance of benefits to be restored to the household when the household asks to have benefits restored over several months - A copy of the overissuance claim if one is used to reduce the amount of restored benefits Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing March 26, 2004

title: 581.1 General Policy
text: Even if it is not currently eligible, a household is entitled to restored benefits when benefits were not authorized and should have been. A household is also entitled to restored benefits when the amount authorized was less than the household was entitled to receive. Restored benefits may be authorized for any of the following reasons: - The CAO incorrectly computed benefits, delayed or denied an application in error, or terminated benefits in error. - Deductible expenses are unverified. The CAO did not allow the household 10 days before certification to verify expenses and processed an application without deductions to meet the 30-day deadline. - A person whose SSI application was pending when the CAO authorized SNAP benefits has since been found eligible for SSI. NOTE: The restoration begins with the date of SSI eligibility or the date of the SNAP application, whichever is later. Benefits are restored if the household becomes categorically eligible or to allow deductions the household is entitled to because of a disabled member. - The Social Security Administration (SSA) or the CAO makes an error on applications handled through joint processing; including losing an application after it was filed with the SSA. - The CAO fails to renew an eligible household that filed a timely renewal and met all eligibility requirements. - A disqualification for intentional program violation is reversed. NOTE: The reversal may be the result of an appeal or court action. A household is not entitled to restored benefits when the loss is due to an unreported change or an error made by the household. Reissued March 1, 2012 , replacing July 15, 2008

title: 581.2 Method of Discovery
text: The CAO must restore benefits that a household is entitled to, regardless of the method of discovery, when: - The household makes a written or oral request; - The CAO discovers a loss of benefits; NOTE: When the CAO discovers the loss, it must automatically restore benefits without requiring the household to request restored benefits . - A fair hearing decision is favorable to the household; or - The household disputes in court a disqualification for intentional program violation and the court reverses the disqualification. Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing April 18, 2006

title: 581.3 Time Limits
text: Benefits are restored only if the loss occurred within 12 months before the earliest of the following dates: - The date the household requests restored benefits or tells the CAO that it believes benefits were lost - The date the household requests a fair hearing or begins court action - The date the CAO discovers or is notified of the loss 7 CFR § 273.17(a)(1)(ii) 7 CFR § 273.17(a)(2)(iii) Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing March 26, 2004

title: 581.4 Computing Lost Benefits
text: The CAO must follow these steps to compute the amount of lost benefits: - Correct the amount of the current benefit, if necessary. - Determine the period for which benefits are to be restored, excluding months before the 12-month limit. - Determine eligibility for each month in which benefits have been lost. NOTE: If the case record does not contain enough information to decide on eligibility, the CAO must tell the household what information is needed. The household is ineligible for restored benefits for any month it does not provide information needed for eligibility. - Compute the amount of lost benefits from the beginning of the restoration period until the error was corrected or the household became ineligible, by: - Computing benefit amounts the household should have received for each month; - Subtracting the monthly benefits amount the household actually received; and - Adding the monthly differences to get the total amount of lost benefits. - Restore the benefit to the household unless there is an outstanding overissuance claim. If there is an overissuance claim, the Office of Inspector General (OIG) must determine the amount of the benefit to be restored.

title: 581.4 Computing Lost Benefits
section_title: 581.41 Offsetting an Overissuance against Lost Benefits
text: If an overissuance claim against a household is unpaid or suspended, the CAO must offset the amount of the restored benefit against the amount due on the claim. The CAO must restore the balance, if any, to the household. The CAO may establish an overissuance for some months when computing the restored benefit. The offset also applies to these overissuances. If the case record contains an overpayment referral, the CAO must send the Offset Overissuance Against Restored Benefits form ( PA 816SP ; see Appendix B of this section ) to the OIG to determine whether there is a balance due on the overissuance claim. The OIG must tell the CAO the benefit amount to be restored. If the CAO completes an overpayment referral, it must use the Automated Restitution Referral and Computation (ARRC) system. Once the claim has been computed, the CAO must send the PA 816SP to the OIG. If a paper PA 189 is required, the CAO must attach the PA 816SP to the paper PA 189 . To offset the overissuance against restored benefits: - The claim must be computed in ARRC; - The amount must be passed to RMS ; or - The CAO must send a paper PA 189 with the PA 816SP . The OIG follows up on claims selected for prosecution review in which the claim amount has not passed to RMS. Reissued March 1, 2012 , replacing July 15, 2008

title: 581_Appendix A
text: RE: Household Name To whom it may concern: The household listed herein is entitled to restoration of SNAP benefits. Because the household is leaving this state, full benefits cannot be restored prior to the effective date of transfer. This letter provides documentation of the additional amount of benefits to be restored to this household and authorizes the receiving state to restore these remaining benefits accordingly. a. Total amount to be restored b. Amount restored by former state c. Amount to be restored by new state (a-b) Is restoration a result of a hearing decision? // Yes // No cc: Household Reissued March 1, 2012 , replacing July 15, 2008

title: 581.5 Client Notice
text: Whether the CAO discovers an error or the household asks for restored benefits , the CAO must notify the household of the following: - The household's entitlement to lost benefits - The amount of benefits to be restored - The amount of any overissuance being offset - The household's right to appeal The CAO must send a notice of ineligibility if requested benefits are denied. Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing March 26, 2004

title: 581.6 Disputed Benefits
text: The household may appeal the amount of restored benefits , the denial of restored benefits, or any action taken by the CAO to restore benefits. The household must file the appeal within 90 days from the date of the notice. Pending a fair hearing decision, the CAO must restore benefits the household was eligible to receive. If the fair hearing decision is favorable to the household, the CAO must restore benefits under the terms of the hearing decision. If the household appeals the CAO decision that it is not entitled to restored benefits, the CAO must not provide restored benefits while waiting for the hearing decision. Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing March 26, 2004

title: 581.7 Method of Restoration
text: The CAO usually authorizes restored benefits in full. This authorization is in addition to the regular monthly benefit issued to a SNAP household . The CAO must use reason code 158 when authorizing restored benefits. The CAO must honor a reasonable request by a household to restore lost benefits in monthly installments. Example: A household asks for restored benefits in monthly installments, because the amount restored is more than the household can use in a reasonable period of time. Reissued March 1, 2012 , replacing August 10, 2005

title: 581.8 Change in Who Is in the Household
section_title: 581.8 Change in Who Is in the Household
text: If the household changes, the CAO must issue restored benefits to the household with the majority of persons who were household members when the loss happened. If the CAO cannot locate the household or cannot determine which household contains the majority, the CAO must restore benefits to the household that contains the person who was the payment name when the loss occurred. If the CAO does not know where the payment name and the majority are, it must restore benefits to the household with the greater number of remaining members, even if it is only one member. Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing March 26, 2004

title: 581.9 Out-of-State Moves
text: If possible, the CAO must restore benefits before a household moves out of Pennsylvania. If a household moved from Pennsylvania but can use the benefits through the Pennsylvania EBT ACCESS card and the Quest national network, the CAO must restore the benefits to the existing EBT account. If a household cannot return to Pennsylvania to use the benefits, does not live in a Quest state, or is not able to use its Pennsylvania EBT ACCESS card, the CAO must send a letter to the SNAP agency in the new state certifying the household's entitlement to restored benefits . - The letter must state whether the restoration is the result of a fair hearing decision. If so, a copy of the hearing decision must be attached. - A copy of the letter must go to the household. - A copy of the letter must be filed in the case record. NOTE: See Appendix A of this section for a sample letter. When a household is entitled to restored benefits but moves without giving a new address, the CAO must keep the information in the case record. The information must be made available upon request from another state agency for up to one year from the month of the move. Reissued March 1, 2012, replacing September 2, 2005

title: Appendix A: Current Geographical Areas Waived
section_title: As of September 1, 2025, The Food and Nutrition Service (FNS) has not approved Pennsylvaniaâs request for a geographic waiver. All counties in Pennsylvania are subject to the Time-Limited Receipt of Benefits.
text: Areas subject to Time Limit (Effective September 1, 2025) * Denotes counties where part or all of the county became subject to time limited benefits on June 1, 2025, for new applicants. - Lancaster County excluding City of Lancaster - Lebanon County excluding City of Lebanon - Montgomery County excluding the Municipality of Norristown - York County excluding York, York Township, and Springettsbury Township

title: Appendix B: Discretionary Exemptions
text: Discretionary exemptions will now be used to exempt the following: - Exempting the following PEERs who meet state employment and training exemption criteria: - Expected to return to work within 60 days (ETP Code 15). An individual may receive this exemption if they: - have recent work history, defined as: were employed within the last six months; or recently completed a SNAP E&T training activity (AC-84, 85, 86, 87, 88, or 91) and are now in a job search activity (AC-89) - were employed within the last six months; or - recently completed a SNAP E&T training activity (AC-84, 85, 86, 87, 88, or 91) and are now in a job search activity (AC-89) - reasonably anticipate resuming work of at least 20 hours per week in the next 60 days; and - do not meet the requirements for EB-21 (see: Chapter 536.25 ) - Non-Chronic Homelessness (ETP Code 16). An individual is non-chronically homeless if they: - have been homeless for less than six months; AND - have not been intermittently homeless in the past two years. NOTE: The CAO must assign ETP Code 26 and Qual Code 10 to an individual who is chronically homeless. - Travel time is two or more hours round-trip from an employment/education training site (ETP Code 19) The following individuals who previously received a discretionary exemption will now receive a federal exemption instead: - Full-Time AmeriCorps VISTA volunteers will now be coded ETP Code 17 and Qual Code 3 and will receive a federal exemption due to working at least 30 hours per week or earning a stipend (in-kind pay) equal to 30 times the minimum wage per week.

title: Appendix B: Discretionary Exemptions
text: - Expedited SNAP recipients who have used less than three countable months and have verification of an exemption or work requirement pending at the time expedited benefits are issued will now be coded ETP Code 23 and Qual Code 16 and will receive a federal exemption for the expedited period. The following individuals who previously received a discretionary exemption will now be subject to the time limit: - Expedited SNAP recipients who have already established they meet no exemption and are not meeting the work requirement at the time expedited benefits are issued will be coded ETP Code 30 and Qual Code 20 and will incur a countable month if their expedited NCE contains a full calendar month. NOTE: Individuals who apply for SNAP on the 2 nd through 15 th days of the month will not incur a countable month in the month of application because their expedited benefit only covers a partial month. Effective June 1, 2025 â October 31, 2025 : Discretionary exemptions were used to exempt the following: - Exempting the following ABAWDs who meet state employment and training exemption criteria: - Full-time VISTA Volunteer (Title I DVSA) (ETP Status 06) - Expected to return to work within 60 days (ETP Status 15) - Travel time is two or more hours round-trip from an employment/education training site (ETP Status 19) - Receiving expedited SNAP only and meets no exemption code (ETP Status 23) - For June 2025 â September 2025 , exempting certain PEERs until their next renewal but no later than October 1, 2025 (for current recipients) who were not eligible for any other exemption and who were not meeting the work requirement in: - Lancaster County excluding the City of Lancaster;

title: Appendix B: Discretionary Exemptions
text: - Lebanon County excluding the City of Lebanon; - Montgomery County excluding the Municipality of Norristown; - York County excluding the City of York, York Township, and Springettsbury Township - Municipalities â Cranberry Township (Butler County), and Hampden Township (Cumberland County) For September 2025 only, exempting all ABAWDs who are not eligible for any other exemption, who are not meeting the work requirement, and who: - were receiving SNAP on August 22, 2025, in an area that was waived at that time; and - were between the ages of 44 â 54, regardless of county of residence NOTE: Individuals who met these temporary additional criteria were assigned SNAP ETP Code 27 and SNAP Qual Code 9 by Headquarters and were not subject to the time limit for the specified temporary period.

title: LIHWAP Water Assistance
text: This document is an English-only policy summary of the LIHWAP handbook, rewritten for retrieval-augmented generation workflows. It focuses on the most important rules for screening, eligibility reasoning, benefit calculation, and verification. The goal is not to reproduce the handbook verbatim. The goal is to make the policy easier for an agent to retrieve, cite, and apply consistently when evaluating whether a household appears eligible for LIHWAP assistance. Primary coverage: Chapters 203, 205, 210, 223, 238, 250, and 278. Primary use case: automated eligibility screening with human-review escalation when facts are incomplete, conflicting, or policy-sensitive. Important limitation: the income limits in this local snapshot reflect the appendix captured in the workspace and should be refreshed before operational deployment. A LIHWAP agent should reason through the case in a fixed order so that eligibility, benefit amount, and missing proof are not mixed together. Determine whether the case is a LIHWAP water crisis case rather than a general utility assistance question. Identify whether the household is applying for drinking water, wastewater, or both. Build the household correctly, including special treatment for excluded members whose income may still count. Confirm Pennsylvania permanent residence and identify temporary-residency problems. Confirm that the household is responsible for the water costs, even if the bill is in another person's name. Check categorical income eligibility first. If categorical eligibility does not apply, calculate countable gross annual income under the LIHWAP rules. Determine whether the crisis can actually be resolved under the program's benefit cap and vendor rules. Return both an eligibility assessment and a missing-verification list. LIHWAP is a temporary federal program that helps low-income households pay drinking water and wastewater bills. The Pennsylvania Department of Human Services administers the program through County Assistance Offices. For agent design, this chapter matters because it defines the program's purpose and explains that LIHWAP is crisis-oriented rather than a general monthly subsidy. LIHWAP serves households with arrearages, termination notices, or terminated drinking water or wastewater service. The program is structured as a crisis grant. If the household has a life-threatening situation, a complete application should be processed within one business day. An agent should classify cases by crisis type and urgency before moving into household and income analysis. Chapter 205: LIHWAP Eligibility Criteria This is the core eligibility chapter and should be treated as the primary policy gate for the agent. The applicant is the main source of information, but the applicant must provide true, complete, and verifiable information. A household must meet the LIHWAP requirements at the time the application is processed. The household must be within the program income limit. The household must be responsible for paying drinking water or wastewater costs to a water provider. The household must permanently live in Pennsylvania. Qualified noncitizens are eligible if they are otherwise eligible. The household is defined as a person or group living together as one economic unit that pays for drinking water or wastewater. A person may be excluded from the applicant household for a given water type if that person already received LIHWAP for the same water type as part of another household, but that person's income may still count. This chapter is essential for RAG because household composition affects both household size and income counting. A strong agent should retrieve these rules whenever the household structure is not straightforward. A household is a person or group of persons who live together as one economic unit and pay for drinking water and wastewater service. Eligibility is based on household composition at the time the application is processed. All household members count for household size unless a specific exclusion applies. Related roomers must be included in the household. If a person living with the applicant already received LIHWAP for the same water type in another household, that person is not counted as a household member for that same water type, but the income still counts. Children in foster care or kinship care may be included or excluded, whichever is more beneficial to the household. If included, their income counts. If excluded, their income does not count. Adopted children and children in a Subsidized Permanent Legal Custodian arrangement are treated as household members. A shared-custody child may be counted in only one household. The first parent to apply may include the child. A household member who is temporarily living elsewhere may still be included if the person has not permanently moved out, but the reason and expected return should be verified. Homeowners may qualify. Renters may qualify only if they are responsible for drinking water or wastewater costs. Renters whose water is included in rent are not eligible. Subsidized housing residents may qualify if they are billed separately for drinking water or wastewater. Temporary visitors, people in institutions, dormitories, fraternity or sorority houses, and boarding homes are not counted as household members. Residence rules are narrower than a simple address check. The key issue is whether the household's permanent home is in Pennsylvania. A resident is a person whose permanent home is in Pennsylvania and who lives there voluntarily and not temporarily for a reason such as a visit, vacation, or education. Temporary residents are not eligible for LIHWAP assistance. If a household is temporarily living somewhere else because of an emergency or a situation beyond its control, the household may still qualify, but it must provide documentation explaining the situation. A household member whose permanent residence is in the LIHWAP household may still be counted even if temporarily living elsewhere, as long as the person has not permanently moved out. Applicants who live in campers or RVs may qualify if the camper or RV is permanently located in Pennsylvania. A household operating a licensed business from the residence may be ineligible if the household claims home utilities as a business deduction and uses 51 percent or more of the home for business purposes. Chapter 238: LIHWAP Program Benefit This chapter answers two distinct questions: what LIHWAP can pay for, and how much it can pay. A retrieval system should keep those questions separate. LIHWAP crisis grants may be used to resolve water emergencies involving lack of water service, arrearages, or imminent loss of water service. Allowable uses include reconnection fees, late payment fees, security deposits, and other charges that a non-LIHWAP customer would also be required to pay. The benefit amount is based on the arrearage amount. Only past-due amounts should be used when determining the benefit amount. If the arrearage is equal to or less than the maximum crisis amount, the benefit should equal the past-due amount. The maximum LIHWAP crisis amount in the captured handbook text is $2,500 per category. If the arrearage exceeds the maximum amount, the CAO must determine whether the vendor will accept the maximum amount to maintain or restore service. If the vendor will not accept the maximum amount, the household may still qualify if it can pay the difference needed to resolve the crisis. If the crisis cannot actually be resolved, the application should be rejected. If drinking water and wastewater are billed by the same vendor, both categories may be authorized up to the maximum amount for each category. A LIHWAP benefit should never create a credit balance on the client's account. Chapter 250: Income Income is one of the most important retrieval areas because the rules involve categorical eligibility, countable income, exclusions, computation periods, and zero-income follow-up. LIHWAP generally uses countable gross income, not net income. Both earned and unearned income are counted unless a specific exclusion applies. Countable income may include wages, salaries, tips, commissions, bonuses, self-employment profit, Social Security benefits, SSI, unemployment compensation, support payments, pensions, cash gifts, and other listed income types. Self-employment is counted based on profit rather than gross receipts, using allowable business costs defined by the policy. Some income is excluded, including TANF, SNAP, certain educational assistance, some volunteer stipends, certain water-cost assistance paid directly to a vendor, and verified loans. If the entire household is made up of members currently receiving SNAP, TANF, or SSI, the household is categorically income eligible. A household may also be categorically income eligible if the same household received LIHEAP in the 2021-2022 or 2022-2023 season, subject to the handbook examples and record structure described in the policy. If a household is not categorically income eligible, the CAO generally uses income from the month before the application and annualizes it. The applicant may request use of the twelve-month period ending in the month before application. If reported income is zero or near zero, the household must explain how it pays for rent or mortgage, food, utilities, and basic living needs. If money is paid directly to the household to cover bills, that money may count as unearned income. Income Limits from the Local Handbook Snapshot The locally captured Appendix A includes the following LIHWAP income limits. These values are useful for a RAG corpus, but they should be treated as time-sensitive policy data. Household size 1: $20,385 Household size 2: $27,465 Household size 3: $34,545 Household size 4: $41,625 Household size 5: $48,705 Household size 6: $55,785 Household size 7: $62,865 Household size 8: $69,945 Household size 9: $77,025 Household size 10: $84,105 The captured appendix states that it was updated on June 20, 2023. A production agent should refresh this appendix from the source handbook before relying on the limits operationally. Chapter 278: Verification This chapter is critical for turning policy reasoning into an actionable case checklist. A good agent should return both a likely eligibility outcome and the missing proof needed to support the case. The applicant must prove key eligibility items such as residence, citizenship or qualified noncitizen status, household income, and other facts needed to decide eligibility. If information in the application conflicts with CIS or e-CIS, the conflicting information must be verified. If the agency learns of changed household circumstances after the application is submitted but before it is processed, the updated information must be used. Items that must be verified include Social Security numbers, earned and unearned income, responsibility for water costs, residence, need for a crisis payment, noncitizen status, and questionable information. All household members must have a Social Security number unless a handbook exception applies, such as a child younger than one year old. Members who do not have or refuse to provide a Social Security number must sign the appropriate affidavit or certification. Acceptable income verification may include pay stubs, benefit award letters, support letters, and automated sources such as CIS, e-CIS, and IEVS. Water responsibility and crisis need may be verified through recent water bills, shutoff notices, arrearage notices, leases, landlord statements, and documented third-party verification. If the household reports zero income, the CAO may require the Zero-Income Statement and further explanation. The CAO must help the applicant obtain proof when necessary. Fields a RAG-Backed Agent Should Extract For practical retrieval and decision support, the agent should normalize case facts into consistent fields. Application date. Water category requested: drinking water, wastewater, or both. Crisis status: arrearage, shutoff notice, terminated service, imminent loss of service, life-threatening situation. Household members and household size. Member-level flags: foster, kinship, adopted, SPLC, shared custody, temporary absence, already received LIHWAP elsewhere, citizen or qualified noncitizen status. Residence facts: Pennsylvania permanent residence, temporary relocation, RV or camper status, home-based business facts. Water-cost responsibility facts: direct bill, landlord bill, lease responsibility, included in rent, separate billing. Income facts: categorical eligibility status, earned income, unearned income, exclusions, zero-income explanation, self-employment profit. Benefit facts: arrearage amount, vendor acceptance of payment, amount needed to reconnect or continue service, whether the crisis can be fully resolved. Verification facts: what has been provided, what is missing, and what conflicts with system data. A RAG system should produce a structured answer even if the user sees natural language. This keeps retrieval, rule application, and auditability aligned. Eligibility assessment: likely eligible, likely ineligible, or needs human review. Reasoning summary tied to the relevant chapter rules. Household determination summary. Income determination summary. Benefit determination summary, including whether the crisis can be resolved under program limits. Missing verification items. Manual-review flags for conflicting, incomplete, or exceptional facts. This summary was created from the LIHWAP handbook pages captured locally in the workspace. It is designed for retrieval and reasoning support rather than as a legal opinion or official replacement for the handbook. For operational use, refresh the handbook content periodically. For high-stakes cases, keep a link from each RAG chunk back to the original handbook page.

title: Transit Relief Pilot
text: This policy supports households with transportation hardship. Applicants should bring proof of address and proof of reduced work hours.
